title: Day 1 · Phase 1 — Setting up the question
bodyHtml:
DAY 1 · Phase 1 — Setting up the question

The Briefing: Something is Coming

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can describe what we currently know and don't know about 2026-XJ, and identify what physics tools I would need to learn more.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Nothing yet — students walk in cold on Day 1.

How today connects: Today IS the Day 1 disclosure. Students receive the NASA briefing, react to it, and begin generating the questions that will drive the year.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–3 min

• Welcome. Norms: this class will be different. We'll be solving a year-long puzzle together. Today is the briefing.

• Hand out the NASA Day 1 Briefing document (one-page handout).
3–10 min

• Students read the briefing SILENTLY for 5 minutes.

• Brief moment to react: this is fiction. The physics is real. We're going to spend the year figuring out what we can about this object.
10–25 min

• Notice and Wonder protocol. In groups of 3-4: what did you NOTICE in the briefing? What do you WONDER?

• Each group writes 3 notices and 5+ wonders on chart paper.

• Gallery walk: students put up their chart paper and visit other groups' lists.
25–40 min

• Full-class discussion: what kinds of QUESTIONS are we generating? Capture them on the board.

• Categorize: "How fast?" "Where?" "When?" "How big?" "What's it made of?" "Can we stop it?"

• Reveal: these questions are going to organize the YEAR. Each unit answers one piece of this puzzle.

• Show the seven unit titles (puzzle questions) — let students see that the year already has a structure.
40–50 min

• Today's anchor question: "How fast and where will it arrive?" — this is Unit 1's puzzle piece.

• Brief preview: to answer this, we need the language of motion. Vocabulary like position, velocity, acceleration. We'll start tomorrow.

• Exit ticket: write ONE question you have about the asteroid that you really want answered this year.

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Read the exit ticket questions tonight. Which questions are physics questions vs. social/political questions? (Both are valid.)

• Note any students who didn't engage at all in the notice/wonder activity — could indicate concern about the premise. Check in privately tomorrow.

• Look for misconceptions in students' wonders: anyone who thinks the asteroid is already visible? Anyone who thinks we already know exactly what it is?

MATERIALS & READING

• NASA Day 1 Briefing handout (one page per student)

• Chart paper for notice/wonder

• Markers

• Display of the seven unit titles (poster or projection)

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Read Chapter 2 (Motion) introduction tonight. We'll start with vocabulary tomorrow.

TEACHER NOTES

TONE MATTERS TODAY. The briefing is dramatic but not panicked. The framing is "we have a year to figure this out" — not "prepare for the end." Some students will engage immediately; some will be skeptical. Both are fine. The skeptics often become the most engaged students by Unit 4. ALSO: today's notice/wonder will generate questions you can return to throughout the year. Save the chart paper. Students will be delighted in May when you point back to a Day 1 question and say "now you can answer that." The framing "this is fiction; the physics is real" should be repeated several times today and across the first week. The classroom culture you're establishing: this is a serious intellectual project.

title: Day 2 · Phase 1 — Setting up the question
bodyHtml:
DAY 2 · Phase 1 — Setting up the question

How Do We Talk About Motion?

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can use precise physics vocabulary (position, distance, displacement) to describe an object's motion, and I can distinguish between scalar and vector quantities.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: An object is approaching Earth. We know roughly its position and velocity, but with significant uncertainty.

How today connects: To predict WHERE the asteroid will be, we need precise language for position and motion. Today builds the vocabulary the year will rely on.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Warm-up question on board: "A student walks from the classroom door to their desk and back. How far did they walk?"

• Collect answers. Most students say something like "twice the distance" or "zero, they ended up in the same place."

• Two correct answers? How can that be?
5–20 min

• Introduce the distinction: DISTANCE = total path length (a scalar). DISPLACEMENT = change in position (a vector — has direction).

• Vocabulary on the board: position, distance, displacement, scalar, vector.

• Practice problems: a runner does laps around a 400m track. After one lap: distance = 400m, displacement = 0. After 1.5 laps: distance = 600m, displacement = 200m (across the field). Students work in pairs.
20–35 min

• Hands-on: each student walks a path described by the teacher. "Walk 5 steps north, 3 steps east, 5 steps south."

• Students measure their own DISTANCE walked vs. their DISPLACEMENT from start.

• Brief whole-class share: who's surprised that distance and displacement can be so different?
35–48 min

• Apply to the asteroid: NASA's data tells us 2026-XJ's CURRENT POSITION (a point in space). To predict its future, we need to know its DISPLACEMENT over time — and the direction matters.

• Show a simplified 2D map: Earth and the asteroid's current position. Discuss: if the asteroid moves 1 million km, does it MATTER which direction? (Yes! The same 1M km moves it toward Earth or away from Earth depending on direction.)

• Quick reasoning: vectors are required for asteroid trajectory prediction. This is why we'll spend the next week on motion vocabulary and graphs.
48–50 min • Exit ticket: "Explain in one sentence why we can't just describe the asteroid's motion as 'distance traveled' — we need displacement."

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Exit ticket: do students articulate the direction issue? If 80%+ get it, move on tomorrow. If not, briefly review at the start of Day 3.

• Walking activity: did students physically struggle to follow vector directions? Some may need extra support with cardinal directions and angles.

• Vocabulary check: by end of class, can students use 'displacement' correctly in a sentence?

MATERIALS & READING

• Whiteboard / chart paper

• Open space for the walking activity (gym, hallway, or rearranged classroom)

• Cardinal direction signs (N/S/E/W) posted around the room

• Practice problem handout (4-5 distance vs displacement problems)

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Read Chapter 2 sections on Position, Velocity, and Vectors (~5 pages). Mark anything confusing.

TEACHER NOTES

This day establishes vocabulary that EVERY subsequent unit will rely on. The vector vs. scalar distinction is one of the deepest in physics — students who get it now will save themselves grief in every unit later. Common confusion: students treat 'displacement' as a synonym for 'distance.' Use the walking activity to make the difference physical. Also: the asteroid connection at the end of class is critical. Students need to leave today understanding that the vocabulary they're learning has a REASON — it's not arbitrary. "We need displacement, not just distance, because the asteroid has DIRECTION" is a sentence students should be able to say by Day 5.

title: Day 3 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 3 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Velocity: Motion with Direction

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can calculate velocity from position-time data, distinguish speed from velocity, and represent motion using a position-time graph.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 2. We have position data for 2026-XJ but need to talk about how it changes over time.

How today connects: Velocity is HOW position changes with time. NASA's trajectory data gives us 2026-XJ's velocity — but we need to know what velocity actually means to use that data.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap from Day 2: distance vs. displacement. Quick check question on board.

• Today's question: "If two cars travel from Fitchburg to Worcester, but one takes 1 hour and the other takes 2 hours, are they moving the same way?"
5–15 min

• Build the speed and velocity definitions from student responses.

• Speed = distance / time (scalar). Velocity = displacement / time (vector).

• Units: m/s (the physics standard). Convert km/h to m/s as a class.

• The asteroid's velocity: ~8,500 m/s. How fast is that in km/h? In mph? (Spoiler: ~19,000 mph. Way faster than any car or commercial plane.)
15–35 min

• INVESTIGATION 1.1 (Match-the-Graph): Vernier motion detector connected to laptop. Display target position-time graphs.

• Students take turns walking in front of the detector to MATCH the displayed graph. (Predict, attempt, observe, refine.)

• Start with simple constant velocity. Build to changes in direction (which require negative velocities).
35–48 min

• Whole-class debrief: what does a position-time graph TELL us?

• Slope of position-time graph = velocity. Steeper = faster.

• Negative slope = moving in the negative direction (away from origin).

• Connect to asteroid: NASA's data could be plotted as a position-time graph. If we know the slope at any point, we know the asteroid's velocity at that point.
48–50 min • Exit ticket: "Sketch a position-time graph for an object moving at constant velocity AWAY from the origin. Label the axes and the slope."

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Match-the-graph success: do students improve over multiple attempts? Students who can't match by end of activity may need extra support.

• Exit ticket sketch: do students label axes correctly? Do they understand that constant velocity = straight line?

• Watch for the misconception that a flat horizontal line means "not moving." (On a position-time graph, that IS not moving — position isn't changing. But many students think it means constant speed.)

MATERIALS & READING

• Vernier motion detectors (1 per 4-5 students)

• Laptops with Logger Pro or Graphical Analysis software

• Display of target graphs (projected or printed)

• Whiteboards for sketching

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Read Hewitt Chapter 2 sections on Velocity and Graphs (~3-4 pages).

TEACHER NOTES

The match-the-graph activity is BELOVED by students. It's also genuinely diagnostic: students who can match graphs can read them; students who can't, can't. Watch for students who confuse "moving fast" with "moving forward" — a steep negative slope means moving fast IN THE NEGATIVE DIRECTION. The asteroid connection is critical today: by end of class, students should see that ALL of NASA's trajectory data is just position-vs-time data at huge scale. We're learning to read the same kind of graph for an asteroid that we're using to walk in front of a motion detector. Same physics, different units.

title: Day 4 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 4 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Velocity-Time Graphs and Vector Thinking

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can interpret a velocity-time graph, decompose a 2D velocity into components, and represent velocity vectors with proportional arrows.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: We now know 2026-XJ's current velocity is 8,500 m/s at 15° toward Earth. But what does "at 15°" really mean?

How today connects: Today we learn that velocity has COMPONENTS. The asteroid's velocity has a part directed toward Earth and a part perpendicular. We need both to predict where it will be.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap: yesterday's position-time graph slope = velocity.

• New question: "What does a velocity-TIME graph look like for the same motion?"
5–15 min

• Introduce velocity-time graphs. Students sketch v-t graphs for several scenarios already displayed as p-t graphs from yesterday.

• Key insights: constant velocity → horizontal line on v-t graph; changing velocity → tilted line.

• Slope of velocity-time graph = acceleration (preview for Day 8). For now, focus on reading the graph itself.
15–35 min

• Vectors as arrows. Draw a velocity vector on the board: an arrow whose LENGTH = magnitude and DIRECTION = direction.

• Practice: draw velocity vectors for: a car going 60 mph east, a plane going 200 mph north, a runner going 5 m/s southwest.

• Now: 2D velocity decomposition. The asteroid moves at 8,500 m/s at 15° toward Earth. Use trigonometry: v_toward_Earth = v·cos(15°) ≈ 8,210 m/s. v_perpendicular = v·sin(15°) ≈ 2,200 m/s.

• Most students don't yet have strong trig — this is intentionally just visual today. We'll use protractors and graphic decomposition tomorrow.
35–48 min

• On graph paper, students draw the asteroid's velocity vector and visually decompose it into toward-Earth and perpendicular components.

• Discuss: in 6 months (the Unit 1 transfer task timeframe), how far does each component move the asteroid? Calculate roughly using rate × time.

• Connect: this is exactly what NASA is doing right now to predict where 2026-XJ will be.
48–50 min • Exit ticket: "Why does it matter that we calculate the components of the asteroid's velocity separately?"

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Vector arrow drawings: are students drawing arrows with proportional length AND correct direction? Both matter.

• Exit ticket: students should articulate that direction-toward-Earth tells us if it will HIT; direction-perpendicular tells us where it will be when it gets here.

• Watch for students who can do the cos/sin calculation but don't understand why. Pure math doesn't help here; the meaning is the physics.

MATERIALS & READING

• Graph paper

• Rulers and protractors

• Calculators

• Vector worksheet (5-6 practice problems with both magnitude and direction)

• Display of velocity-time graphs

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Read Hewitt Chapter 2 sections on Vectors and Vector Components (~4 pages). This is where the math starts to come.

TEACHER NOTES

VECTOR THINKING IS THE LOAD-BEARING SKILL OF THIS UNIT. Today is the first day vectors are explicitly introduced, but they'll appear in every subsequent day. Don't rush. For CPA students with weak math, the trig is a real obstacle. Strategies: (1) Start with VISUAL decomposition on graph paper — students literally draw the arrow and measure its components with a ruler. (2) Build to TRIGONOMETRIC decomposition only after the visual is secure. (3) Provide a "recipe card" with the trig formulas — students don't need to derive cos and sin from scratch. The asteroid connection makes the vector work meaningful. Without it, students see vectors as math homework. With it, they see vectors as the tool for answering "will it hit us?"

title: Day 5 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 5 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Predicting Position Using Velocity

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can use velocity and time to predict an object's future position, and I can express uncertainty in a prediction.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: We know 2026-XJ's current position and velocity (with components calculated yesterday). Tomorrow we get more disclosure — but today we predict.

How today connects: TODAY IS THE FIRST DAY STUDENTS DO REAL ASTEROID PHYSICS. Using yesterday's velocity components, students predict where 2026-XJ will be in 6 months.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap: velocity = displacement / time. So displacement = velocity × time.

• If we know the asteroid's velocity now and assume it stays constant, we can predict where it will be later.
5–25 min

• Set up the prediction problem on the board:

• - Current position: 1.2 × 10⁸ km from Earth.

• - Current velocity components (from Day 4): v_toward_Earth ≈ 8,210 m/s, v_perpendicular ≈ 2,200 m/s.

• - Time forward: 6 months = ~1.6 × 10⁷ seconds.

• Students calculate the displacement in each direction over 6 months. Then add to the current position to predict the new position.

• Big numbers warning: this requires unit conversion (km to m) and scientific notation. Walk through it carefully.
25–40 min

• Discuss the prediction: where will 2026-XJ be in 6 months? Roughly 0.9 × 10⁸ km from Earth, but offset by the perpendicular motion.

• Critical question: HOW SURE are we? The position data has uncertainty (±50,000 km). The velocity data has uncertainty (±200 m/s).

• Calculate the range of possible positions in 6 months given those uncertainties. Sketch the "window" of possible locations.

• Reality: the actual uncertainty is BIGGER than what we calculate, because we assumed constant velocity (the Sun's gravity will alter it — Unit 2's problem).
40–50 min

• Whole-class reflection: this is REAL physics being done by NASA right now (in 2026). The uncertainties they calculate are the basis for whether we deploy a deflection mission.

• Foreshadow Unit 1 transfer task (Days 21-22): students will do this kind of prediction independently with new data.

• Exit ticket: "In your own words, why does our prediction have a 'window' of possible positions instead of a single exact location?"

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Calculation check: did students convert units correctly? Watch for km vs. m errors — this is the most common mistake.

• Uncertainty reasoning: do students understand that 'uncertainty' isn't 'we're guessing' but 'measurements have inherent limits'?

• Exit ticket: do students articulate that data has noise, and that noise propagates through calculations?

MATERIALS & READING

• Calculators

• Graph paper for sketching the prediction window

• Asteroid Position Worksheet (template with the calculation steps)

• Display of the asteroid data from Days 1-4

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Re-read Hewitt Chapter 2's section on equations of motion. Practice problems 1-5 at end of chapter.

TEACHER NOTES

TODAY IS THE FIRST 'REAL' ASTEROID DAY. Students see that physics they've learned for 4 days now lets them do something concrete. The unit conversions and scientific notation will be painful for CPA students with weak math — provide a worked example on the board. Don't let the math obstruct the meaning. The uncertainty discussion is critical: students need to internalize that ALL measurements have uncertainty, and that this is not a flaw but a feature of how science works. Strong students will see that even with perfect uncertainty propagation, our prediction is still approximate because we assumed constant velocity. That's a Unit 2 foreshadow worth naming explicitly.

title: Day 6 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 6 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Vector Addition and Decomposition

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can add two vectors graphically (tip-to-tail) and decompose a vector into components using simple trigonometry.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 5. Today builds the math infrastructure we needed yesterday but used loosely.

How today connects: Vector math is the language of trajectory prediction. Every unit from here forward will use it. Today we make sure we can do it.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap: yesterday we decomposed the asteroid's velocity into two components. Today we practice that skill explicitly.

• Set up: vector addition and decomposition are TOOLS. Today is a skill-building day.
5–25 min

• Graphical vector addition: tip-to-tail method. Draw two vectors on the board. Students copy and add.

• Practice problems with provided vectors: students draw, add tip-to-tail, measure the resultant.

• Examples that connect to the asteroid: "If 2026-XJ's velocity has a 8,210 m/s component toward Earth AND a 2,200 m/s component perpendicular, what's the resultant velocity?" (Tip-to-tail or Pythagorean: ≈ 8,500 m/s.)
25–42 min

• Vector decomposition with trig (formal version). Hand out the Vector Recipe Card: given a vector with magnitude V and angle θ from a reference axis, the components are V·cos(θ) and V·sin(θ).

• Practice problems. Students work in pairs.

• Warning: a lot of CPA students will need explicit instruction on which trig function to use. Reinforce: the COMPONENT ADJACENT to the angle uses cos; the OPPOSITE uses sin. (Watch for the common error of mixing them up.)
42–50 min

• Mini-quiz (3 problems, 5 minutes): one vector addition, one decomposition, one applied to the asteroid.

• Self-check before turning in: did you draw a diagram? Did you use the right trig function? Did you include units?

• Exit ticket: "What's the difference between a vector's MAGNITUDE and its COMPONENTS?"

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Mini-quiz: who can decompose a vector correctly? This is the diagnostic skill check.

• Tip-to-tail diagrams: are students drawing arrows proportionally? Are vectors connected nose-to-tail?

• Watch for students who use formulas without understanding. Probe with: 'Why did you use cosine for that component?'

MATERIALS & READING

• Graph paper (every student)

• Rulers and protractors

• Calculators

• Vector Recipe Card handout (one per student, laminated if possible)

• Vector Practice Worksheet (8-10 problems)

• Mini-quiz (3 problems)

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Hewitt Appendix B (Vectors) — careful read. Practice problems at end of appendix.

TEACHER NOTES

THIS DAY IS CRITICAL FOR CPA STUDENTS WITH WEAK MATH. The Vector Recipe Card is your friend — give it to them, let them use it, and don't apologize. Mastery of vector decomposition is required for everything ahead, but it doesn't have to be derived from first principles. Many students will need to repeatedly look up cos vs. sin even by Unit 4. That's fine. What matters is they can DO IT, not that they have it memorized. Common errors today: (1) using the wrong trig function, (2) not drawing diagrams, (3) calculating magnitudes correctly but losing direction, (4) not labeling units. The mini-quiz is the formative check — students who get all three problems right are ready for Day 7; students who get fewer than 2 need follow-up support.

title: Day 7 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 7 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Acceleration: When Velocity Changes

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can define acceleration as the rate of change of velocity, calculate acceleration from velocity-time data, and identify situations where an object is accelerating.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: We've been treating 2026-XJ's velocity as constant. But that's an approximation — gravity is going to change it. Acceleration is what describes velocity changes.

How today connects: Acceleration explains why orbits work — gravity continuously accelerates objects toward what they're orbiting. Today's tools will become essential in Unit 2.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Question on board: "A car goes from 0 mph to 60 mph in 6 seconds. What's its acceleration?"

• Collect intuitions. Many students will say "10 mph per second." Good — that's the right answer.
5–15 min

• Formalize acceleration: a = Δv / Δt. Units: m/s² (read as "meters per second per second" or "meters per second squared").

• Calculate the car's acceleration in m/s²: 60 mph ≈ 27 m/s, so a ≈ 4.5 m/s².

• Compare: Earth's gravity gives a ≈ 9.8 m/s² (free fall). The car has roughly half of g.

• Acceleration is a VECTOR — has direction. An object slowing down has negative acceleration (in the direction of motion).
15–35 min

• Velocity-time graph practice. Show several v-t graphs; students identify whether the object is accelerating, decelerating, or moving at constant velocity.

• Calculate acceleration from v-t graph slope. Walk through several examples with students.

• Constant acceleration problem-solving: equations of motion (v = v₀ + at, x = x₀ + v₀t + ½at²). Hand out the equations as a reference; don't make students derive them today.
35–48 min

• Apply to the asteroid: at its current distance from Earth (1.2 × 10⁸ km), Earth's gravity gives an acceleration of < 0.001 m/s². Calculate using g = GM/r². Confirm: that's why we treated velocity as constant in Day 5's prediction.

• Foreshadowing: as 2026-XJ gets closer, gravity strengthens (inverse-square). The acceleration becomes significant. Unit 2 will handle this fully.

• For now: the model 'constant velocity' is approximate but useful for far-away objects.
48–50 min • Exit ticket: "A car traveling 30 m/s decelerates to 0 m/s in 5 seconds. What's its acceleration? Show your work."

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Exit ticket: did students compute -6 m/s² (correctly negative, since the car is slowing)? Or did they answer +6?

• v-t graph interpretation: did students correctly identify acceleration from slope?

• Constant-acceleration equations: who can use them confidently? Who needs the formula sheet in front of them?

MATERIALS & READING

• Velocity-time graph worksheet (varied scenarios)

• Equations of Motion handout (constant acceleration)

• Calculators

• Whiteboards for problem-solving

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Read Hewitt Chapter 3 (Linear Motion) introduction and acceleration sections (~4-5 pages).

TEACHER NOTES

Acceleration is conceptually subtle. Many students think acceleration means "speeding up." Use the decelerating car example to break that. Also: many students struggle with the units 'm/s²' — explain it as 'each second, your velocity changes by [a] m/s.' For the asteroid connection today: the calculation g = GM/r² at the asteroid's distance will give a tiny number, confirming our constant-velocity approximation. Students should be comforted by this — Day 5's prediction wasn't wrong; it was a good approximation for the regime we're in. This is a Unit 2 setup: we'll need to retire this approximation as 2026-XJ approaches Earth.

title: Day 8 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 8 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Constant Acceleration Problem-Solving

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can solve problems involving constant acceleration using the equations of motion, and I can identify the correct equation for a given scenario.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 7. Today's a skill-building day for the math we'll use in Unit 2.

How today connects: Real asteroids accelerate as they approach Earth. The skills built today are the math infrastructure for that.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min • Recap acceleration. Quick problem: A skateboarder accelerates from 2 m/s to 5 m/s in 2 seconds. Acceleration?
5–20 min

• Equations of motion review (constant acceleration). Three main equations:

• - v = v₀ + at (velocity)

• - x = x₀ + v₀t + ½at² (position)

• - v² = v₀² + 2a(x - x₀) (velocity in terms of position, no time)

• Identifying which equation to use: depends on what's given and what's asked. Walk through 2-3 examples as a class.
20–40 min

• Students work in pairs on problem set (6-8 problems of increasing difficulty). Examples:

• - A car braking from highway speed. How far does it travel?

• - A ball thrown straight up. How high does it go?

• - A bullet train accelerating from rest. How long until it reaches cruising speed?

• Teacher circulates, helping with stuck pairs.
40–48 min

• Share-out: a couple of pairs present their solutions to the class. Whole-class discusses approach.

• Synthesize: 'How do I choose the right equation?' Strategy: list what's given, list what's asked, find the equation that connects them.
48–50 min • Exit ticket: a one-equation problem from the day's set. Self-check: did I include units? Did I show my work?

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Problem set work: which problems are pairs struggling with? Note for follow-up.

• Share-out quality: are students reasoning about WHY they chose an equation, or just plugging in?

• Exit ticket: clean execution of one problem.

MATERIALS & READING

• Equations of Motion handout (constant acceleration)

• Problem set worksheet (6-8 problems with increasing difficulty)

• Calculators

• Whiteboards for pair work

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Hewitt Chapter 3 — review the worked examples and try problems 3-7.

TEACHER NOTES

Skill-building day. Math will be hard for CPA students; pacing is intentional. Strategy: do the FIRST problem fully on the board, then let pairs work the rest. The 'pair work' format reduces math anxiety — students help each other through the algebra. For students with strong math, push them to think about WHY a given equation applies. For students with weaker math, give them the equations and let them practice using them. Conceptual understanding can come with practice. Common error today: students using the wrong equation because they didn't list what's given/asked first. Build the habit of starting every problem with that step.

title: Day 9 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 9 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Newton's 1st Law: Inertia

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can explain Newton's 1st Law in my own words and identify everyday examples of inertia.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 8. Today we shift from describing motion to explaining WHY motion changes.

How today connects: Newton's 1st Law explains why 2026-XJ keeps moving at nearly constant velocity through space — there's no friction in space. That's good news for predictability; bad news for stopping it.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Question: "Why does a hockey puck slide for a long time on ice, but a brick slid on the floor stops quickly?"

• Collect intuitions. Most students will say "friction." Good.
5–20 min

• Aristotle vs. Galileo. Aristotle thought objects naturally come to rest. Galileo showed (mostly through thought experiments) that objects in motion stay in motion UNLESS something stops them.

• Newton's 1st Law: 'An object in motion stays in motion, and an object at rest stays at rest, unless acted on by a net force.'

• Define INERTIA: an object's resistance to change in motion. Inertia is related to mass.
20–35 min

• Inertia demos: tablecloth pull (pull a tablecloth from under a stack of cups; cups stay). Coin and card on a finger (flick the card; coin drops into a glass).

• Each demo: students predict, observe, explain using inertia.

• Discuss: in a car suddenly stopping, why do passengers slide forward? Inertia. (This is why we wear seatbelts.)
35–48 min

• Apply to the asteroid: 2026-XJ is moving through SPACE. There's no air resistance, no friction. By Newton's 1st Law, it would keep going at constant velocity FOREVER — unless something pushes it.

• The only thing pushing it is GRAVITY (from the Sun, mostly, plus weaker pulls from Earth and Moon).

• So: the asteroid keeps doing what it's doing. To CHANGE its trajectory, we need a FORCE.

• This sets up the next several days: forces.
48–50 min • Exit ticket: "Explain why a deflection mission must apply a FORCE to the asteroid — what does Newton's 1st Law tell us about why the asteroid keeps coming?"

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Exit ticket: do students articulate that Newton's 1st Law means objects don't change motion without a force?

• Demo predictions: do students predict correctly, or are they surprised? Surprise is a learning opportunity.

• Vocabulary check: 'inertia' and 'net force' — can students use both correctly?

MATERIALS & READING

• Tablecloth and stack of plastic cups (for the tablecloth pull demo)

• Coin and index card (for the coin drop demo)

• Other inertia demos as time permits

• Whiteboard

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Read Hewitt Chapter 4 (Newton's First Law and Inertia) — the WHOLE chapter is short. Excellent reading.

TEACHER NOTES

The DEMOS are the day. Inertia is hard to TEACH; it's easy to SEE. Multiple demos with class predictions are the way. The asteroid connection today is critical: 2026-XJ keeps coming because of Newton's 1st Law. Stopping it requires a FORCE. This is the setup for everything ahead — without forces, no deflection. Students who internalize this on Day 9 are well-positioned for Days 10-15.

title: Day 10 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 10 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Forces: What Pushes and Pulls

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can identify different types of forces in a scenario (gravity, normal, friction, applied, tension), and I can represent forces with arrows.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 9. The asteroid keeps coming because no force is stopping it. Today we learn the language of force.

How today connects: Every force on the asteroid changes its trajectory slightly. Solar gravity is the biggest. Earth's gravity becomes significant as it approaches. To plan deflection, we need to add a force we control.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap Newton's 1st Law: a force is required to change motion.

• Today: what IS a force? How do we describe and measure it?
5–20 min

• Force = a push or a pull. Measured in NEWTONS (N). 1 N is about the weight of a small apple.

• Common forces: gravity (always pulling toward center of large body), normal force (surface pushing on you), friction (resisting motion), tension (in ropes/strings), applied force (whatever you push).

• Forces are VECTORS. Each force has both magnitude (in N) and direction.

• Demonstrate with a force sensor: pull on it with your hand and watch the reading. Push with your hand and watch.
20–40 min

• Identifying forces in scenarios. For each scenario, list all forces and draw them as arrows on a sketch:

• - A book resting on a desk. (Forces: gravity down, normal up.)

• - A car going down a road. (Gravity, normal, friction, applied/engine.)

• - A person hanging from a rope. (Gravity, tension.)

• Practice in pairs with several scenarios.
40–48 min

• Apply to the asteroid: list the forces on 2026-XJ right now.

• Solar gravity (toward Sun): strong, dominant force.

• Earth's gravity (toward Earth): tiny right now, but growing.

• Other planetary gravity: negligible.

• Friction: zero (space).

• Solar radiation pressure: very small but exists.

• Discuss: which forces are humans able to add to this picture? (A kinetic impactor adds an applied force. A nuclear option adds a brief but enormous applied force.)
48–50 min • Exit ticket: "List all the forces acting on a car coasting down a hill (no engine power, no brakes)."

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Force identification: do students correctly identify ALL forces in a scenario? Common errors: missing gravity, missing normal force, including non-existent forces (e.g., 'momentum' isn't a force).

• Vector drawing: arrows drawn proportionally to magnitude and in correct direction?

• Exit ticket: complete force list with arrows?

MATERIALS & READING

• Vernier force sensor (1, for demo)

• Force Identification Worksheet (5-6 scenarios)

• Whiteboards for sketching

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Read Hewitt Chapter 5 (Newton's Second Law) introduction and force sections (~4 pages).

TEACHER NOTES

The vocabulary today is the foundation for FREE BODY DIAGRAMS (Days 16-17). Make sure students can identify forces before they try to draw FBDs. Common confusion: students think 'velocity is a force' or 'inertia is a force.' Inertia is RESISTANCE to force; velocity is a result of past forces; they're not forces themselves. The asteroid force list at end of class is critical: students should see that adding a human-controlled force is the ONLY way to deflect the asteroid. That's the whole game.

title: Day 11 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 11 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Newton's 2nd Law: F = ma (Vary Mass)

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can predict and verify how an object's acceleration changes when its mass changes at constant force.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 10. We're learning the relationship between force, mass, and acceleration.

How today connects: 2026-XJ has mass ~3.5 × 10¹¹ kg. To change its acceleration (= deflect it), we need either a huge force or a small force over a long time. F = ma is the math behind every deflection strategy.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap: forces cause changes in motion (Newton's 1st Law). But HOW MUCH does a force change motion? That's Newton's 2nd Law.

• Today: we'll DERIVE F = ma from data. Not from a textbook — from our own data.
5–15 min

• Set up Investigation 1.2 (Vary Mass at Constant Force).

• Setup: dynamics cart on track. Connected by string over a pulley to a hanging mass. Hanging mass creates a constant force on the cart.

• Motion detector at end of track measures cart's velocity.

• Variable: cart mass (add weights). Constant: force (same hanging mass throughout).
15–40 min

• Students run the experiment in groups of 3-4.

• For each cart mass (3-4 different masses): release cart, capture v-t graph, extract acceleration from slope.

• Record results in a table: mass vs. acceleration.

• Plot acceleration vs. mass. (1/mass vs. acceleration may reveal the linear relationship better — depends on which graph students recognize.)
40–48 min

• Whole-class discussion of results: what do you SEE in the data?

• Strong students: acceleration is INVERSELY proportional to mass. Smaller mass → bigger acceleration.

• Mathematical relationship: a ∝ 1/m (or equivalently, m·a is approximately constant).

• Connect to F = ma: at constant F, a = F/m, so a is inversely proportional to m.
48–50 min • Exit ticket: "If you doubled the mass of the cart but kept the force the same, what would happen to the acceleration?"

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Lab data: are students collecting data carefully? Are values reasonable?

• Graphing: can students plot data and identify the trend?

• Exit ticket: students should answer 'acceleration would halve.'

MATERIALS & READING

• Vernier dynamics cart kits (1 per group of 4)

• Tracks, motion detectors, hanging masses, string, pulley

• Logger Pro or Graphical Analysis software on laptops

• Lab handout with data table and graph template

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Read Hewitt Chapter 5 sections on Newton's Second Law (~4 pages). Pay attention to the worked examples.

TEACHER NOTES

TODAY IS THE FIRST 'DERIVE PHYSICS FROM DATA' DAY of the year. Students should leave with the felt understanding that physics laws come from EXPERIMENT, not from textbooks. Watch for groups that skip steps or fudge their data — push back gently. The graphing step is harder than it sounds: some students will struggle with 'inversely proportional.' If groups can't see the trend, suggest plotting 1/m vs. a, which should give a linear relationship. The asteroid connection today: the asteroid's mass is HUGE (~3.5 × 10¹¹ kg). For any reasonable force we can apply, the acceleration will be TINY. This is the basis for understanding why deflection is so hard — and why long lead times are critical.

title: Day 12 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 12 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Newton's 2nd Law: F = ma (Vary Force)

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can predict and verify how an object's acceleration changes when the force on it changes at constant mass.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 11. We've established that acceleration depends on mass; today we test how it depends on force.

How today connects: Yesterday's data showed acceleration depends on mass. Today's will show it also depends on force. Combined: F = ma. This is the equation that governs every deflection mission.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap Day 11: a is inversely proportional to m at constant F.

• Question for today: What if we hold MASS constant and vary FORCE?
5–15 min

• Set up Investigation 1.3 (Vary Force at Constant Mass).

• Same dynamics cart setup as Day 11. But now: cart mass stays the same (no added weights). Variable: hanging mass on the pulley (which sets the force).

• Predict: what do you think will happen as hanging mass increases?
15–40 min

• Run the experiment in groups of 3-4.

• For each hanging mass: release cart, measure acceleration from v-t graph.

• Hint: hanging weight (in Newtons) ≈ m × g, where m is the hanging mass in kg and g ≈ 9.8 m/s². For a 50g (0.05 kg) hanging mass: F ≈ 0.49 N.

• Record force vs. acceleration data.
40–48 min

• Plot acceleration vs. force. Should be roughly LINEAR with positive slope. Slope = 1/m (where m is cart mass).

• Combine with Day 11 results: a ∝ F at constant m AND a ∝ 1/m at constant F. Therefore a ∝ F/m, or equivalently F = ma.

• Newton's Second Law has been derived from data. Celebrate this — they figured it out themselves.
48–50 min • Exit ticket: "If you doubled the force but kept the mass the same, what would happen to the acceleration?"

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Lab data quality: are slopes positive? Are values reasonable?

• Synthesis of Days 11 and 12: do students see how the two experiments combine into F = ma?

• Exit ticket: students should answer 'acceleration would double.'

MATERIALS & READING

• Same as Day 11: Vernier dynamics cart kits, tracks, motion detectors, hanging masses, pulleys, laptops

• Lab handout with data table and graph template

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Re-read Hewitt Chapter 5. Try problems 1, 2, and 5 to apply F = ma.

TEACHER NOTES

TODAY IS WHEN F = ma BECOMES REAL. Students see that the equation isn't arbitrary; it's what their data shows. The combined synthesis ('we just derived Newton's 2nd Law') is a real moment — name it. The connection to deflection: now that we have F = ma, students can do real deflection calculations. For 2026-XJ's mass (3.5 × 10¹¹ kg), an applied force of, say, 1 million N gives an acceleration of only ~3 × 10⁻⁶ m/s². That's tiny. But over months or years, even a tiny acceleration adds up to a meaningful Δv. This sets up the energy/momentum reasoning of later units.

title: Day 13 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 13 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

F = ma in Practice

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can use F = ma to solve problems involving force, mass, and acceleration.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 12. F = ma is now our central equation.

How today connects: Today is skill-building. The asteroid problems we'll do in Units 2-4 all use F = ma.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min • Recap: F = ma, derived from experiment. Quick check: "What's the acceleration of a 5 kg object pushed with a 10 N force?"
5–15 min

• Vocabulary check: NET force (the SUM of all forces, accounting for direction). F = ma uses NET force, not just any one force.

• Example: a box being pulled forward with 20 N applied while friction resists with 5 N. Net force = 15 N. With m = 5 kg, a = 3 m/s².
15–40 min

• Problem set practice. Worksheet with 6-8 problems:

• - Simple F = ma problems (given F and m, find a)

• - Reverse problems (given a and m, find F)

• - Net force problems (multiple forces; students compute net first)

• - One application problem: 'A 1500 kg car needs to decelerate from 30 m/s to 0 m/s in 5 seconds. What net force on it?'

• Students work in pairs. Teacher circulates.
40–48 min

• A couple pairs present their solutions on the board. Class discusses.

• Discuss: in real situations, friction is the resistance force. Driving forward means engine force > friction force. Stopping means brakes (friction) > engine force or coasting (only friction).
48–50 min • Exit ticket: One F = ma problem. Self-check: did I find NET force first?

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Problem set work: who's struggling? Note for follow-up.

• Net force concept: do students always start with 'list all forces'?

• Exit ticket: clean execution showing net force calculation first.

MATERIALS & READING

• F = ma practice worksheet (6-8 problems)

• Calculators

• Whiteboards

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Practice problems at end of Hewitt Chapter 5. Try 3-5 more.

TEACHER NOTES

Pure practice day. F = ma is the most-used equation in mechanics. Today is about FLUENCY. Common error: students forget that F is NET force. Build the habit: every problem starts with 'list the forces; find the net.' This habit will save them on Days 16-17 (FBDs) and in every problem ahead.

title: Day 14 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 14 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Newton's 3rd Law: Action and Reaction

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can identify Newton's 3rd Law force pairs and explain why they don't cancel each other.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 13. We've covered force and acceleration. Today: what happens when two objects interact.

How today connects: When a deflection spacecraft hits the asteroid, the asteroid pushes back on the spacecraft just as hard. Newton's 3rd Law is built into kinetic impactor mission design.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Famous riddle: 'A horse pulls a cart. The cart pulls back on the horse with equal force. So how does the cart ever move?'

• Let students argue for 2 minutes. Collect approaches.
5–15 min

• Resolution of the riddle: the horse-cart pair has internal forces (horse-cart, cart-horse) AND external forces (ground pushing on the horse's feet).

• Newton's 3rd Law: forces come in pairs. If A pushes B with force F, then B pushes A with force F in the opposite direction.

• KEY: the two forces in a 3rd-law pair act on DIFFERENT OBJECTS. They don't cancel because they're not on the same object.
15–35 min

• Investigation 1.4 (Tug-of-War with Force Sensors).

• Two Vernier dual-range force sensors connected by a hook or short rope. Both display on one laptop.

• Demo: two students pull. Watch the readings — they're ALWAYS equal in magnitude, opposite in sign.

• Try various scenarios: hard pull vs. soft pull, one student MUCH stronger than the other, jerky pulls. Readings always match.

• Discussion: what does this mean? The force one student exerts on the other equals what the other exerts on the first. Newton's 3rd Law confirmed by data.
35–48 min

• Apply to asteroid: when a DART-style spacecraft hits 2026-XJ, the asteroid pushes back on the spacecraft just as hard.

• Both objects experience momentum change. The asteroid is so massive that its velocity change is tiny; the spacecraft (much lighter) experiences a huge velocity change (it stops/disintegrates).

• Discuss: what does this teach us? You can deflect a massive object by hitting it with a much smaller object — but only if you can survive the collision intact. (Often you don't; that's fine, you just need to transfer momentum.)
48–50 min • Exit ticket: "When you push against a wall, what pushes back on you with equal force?"

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Tug-of-war discussion: do students see that the force sensors confirm Newton's 3rd Law?

• Common error check: do students still think 'the stronger student' applies more force? (No — they're equal at any instant.)

• Exit ticket: students should answer 'the wall.'

MATERIALS & READING

• Vernier dual-range force sensors (2, for the demo)

• Short rope or hook for connecting the sensors

• Laptop displaying both sensor readings

• Whiteboard for diagrams of force pairs

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Read Hewitt Chapter 6 (Newton's Third Law) — short chapter. Excellent reading.

TEACHER NOTES

Newton's 3rd Law is COUNTER-INTUITIVE. Students intuitively believe that 'the stronger person pulls harder.' The force sensor data DISSOLVES that intuition — they see that no matter who is 'winning' the tug-of-war, both sides feel equal force. The deeper insight: tug-of-war is decided not by who pulls hardest, but by friction with the floor — i.e., by the EXTERNAL forces, not the internal force pair. This is the same insight that resolves the horse-cart riddle. The asteroid connection: kinetic impactor missions work BECAUSE of Newton's 3rd Law. The spacecraft transfers its momentum to the asteroid by the law of equal-and-opposite forces.

title: Day 15 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 15 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Free Body Diagrams Part 1

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can draw a free body diagram showing all forces on a single object, with arrows proportional to force magnitudes.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 14. We've covered all three Newton's laws. Now we draw them.

How today connects: FBDs are the visual language of force. To analyze any asteroid deflection scenario, we need FBDs.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Today: free body diagrams (FBDs). The VISUAL way to represent forces on an object.

• Why FBDs? Once you can draw the forces, you can apply F = ma.
5–25 min

• FBD Rules:

• 1. Draw just the object (or a dot representing it).

• 2. Draw an arrow for EACH force acting on the object.

• 3. Arrows point in the DIRECTION of the force.

• 4. Arrow LENGTH should be proportional to force magnitude.

• 5. Label each arrow (F_gravity, F_normal, F_friction, etc.)

• 6. ONLY draw forces ON the object. NOT forces the object causes on other things.

• Walk through 3-4 examples on the board: book on desk; person standing; box being pushed.
25–43 min

• Students practice FBDs on whiteboards. Scenarios provided one at a time:

• - Book resting on a table

• - Box being pushed horizontally on rough floor

• - Person walking forward

• - Car going around a curve

• - A ball thrown up, at the peak of its flight (only one force — gravity)

• After each, whole-class check. Common errors corrected.
43–50 min

• Connect to asteroid: draw an FBD for 2026-XJ as it approaches Earth. Forces: gravity (toward Sun, dominant), gravity (toward Earth, weaker), gravity (other bodies, negligible).

• If a deflection spacecraft hits it: add an applied force in the direction of the impact.

• Exit ticket: Draw an FBD for a box being PULLED across a floor by a rope, with friction resisting motion.

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Whiteboard FBDs: are students including all forces? Drawing arrows proportionally?

• Common errors: missing normal force, missing friction, adding 'momentum' or 'inertia' as forces (they're not).

• Exit ticket FBD: complete, labeled, with proportional arrows?

MATERIALS & READING

• Whiteboards and dry-erase markers (1 per student)

• FBD Scenarios handout (5-6 scenarios for practice)

• Display of common errors (anti-examples) for discussion

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Re-read Hewitt Chapter 5 sections on free body diagrams (if present in your edition; otherwise refer to teacher-provided notes).

TEACHER NOTES

FBDs are the central VISUAL skill of mechanics. Students who can draw good FBDs can solve almost any force problem. Today is JUST about the diagram — no calculations yet. Common errors: (1) missing the normal force, (2) drawing applied forces in wrong direction, (3) including forces the object exerts on OTHER objects (e.g., the box pushes on the floor — that's a force on the floor, not the box; don't include it on the box's FBD). The practice scenarios are intentionally varied to build flexibility.

title: Day 16 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 16 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Free Body Diagrams Part 2 — Net Force from FBDs

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can compute the net force on an object from its FBD, including for inclined surfaces.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 15. Yesterday we drew FBDs. Today we use them to find net force.

How today connects: Once we have an FBD, F_net = ma tells us the acceleration. This is the bridge from picture to prediction.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min • Recap: FBD shows all forces. F_net is the VECTOR SUM of those forces.
5–20 min

• 1D scenarios first: forces all along one axis. Net force = sum of forces in positive direction MINUS sum in negative direction.

• Example: a box pulled with 30 N applied force, friction = 10 N. Net force = 20 N forward.

• Then: F_net = ma → a = F_net / m. If m = 5 kg, a = 4 m/s².
20–40 min

• 2D scenarios (incline). Block on a tilted surface.

• Use vector decomposition (from Day 6) to split gravity into 'down the incline' and 'into the incline' components.

• Identify all forces on the block (gravity, normal, friction). Find net force along the incline direction.

• Walk through ONE complete example on the board. Then students practice with similar problems.
40–48 min

• Connect to asteroid: 2026-XJ is in deep space, far from any surface. So its FBD is simple: just gravity vectors from various bodies.

• But a deflection spacecraft IN ORBIT around Earth has gravity, plus thrust from its engine. FBDs are essential to plan its trajectory.
48–50 min • Exit ticket: Find net force on a 10 kg box on a horizontal floor, pushed with 50 N forward, with friction = 15 N.

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Inclined plane problems: are students decomposing gravity correctly? This is the hardest skill of the unit.

• Net force calculations: are students vector-adding properly?

• Exit ticket: students should answer F_net = 35 N forward.

MATERIALS & READING

• FBD + Net Force worksheet (5 problems, including 2 inclines)

• Vector Recipe Card (reference from Day 6)

• Whiteboards

• Calculators

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Review Hewitt Chapter 5 — especially worked examples involving inclined surfaces.

TEACHER NOTES

INCLINED PLANES ARE HARD. For CPA students with weak math, vector decomposition on an incline is a serious challenge. Strategies: (1) Walk through ONE complete incline problem step-by-step on the board before students try alone. (2) Provide the Vector Recipe Card with the standard incline decomposition: gravity-along-incline = mg·sin(θ); gravity-into-incline = mg·cos(θ). (3) Build comfort with the geometry through repetition. The asteroid is mostly free of contact forces (it's in space), so inclines won't show up in the asteroid scenarios directly. But spacecraft on Earth's surface (during launch) do involve inclines. And the GENERAL skill of decomposing forces into components will appear in Unit 2 (gravity on the asteroid resolves into components).

title: Day 17 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 17 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Friction: The Force That Resists Motion

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can explain friction as a force that depends on normal force and surface properties, and I can calculate kinetic and static friction.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 16. The asteroid is in space — no friction. But we live in a world full of friction. Understanding it is essential for everyday physics.

How today connects: There's no friction in space. This is GOOD for deflection (a small thrust adds up), and BAD for impact (no friction to slow the asteroid).

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Question: why is it easier to slide a heavy box ONCE you've started moving it than to start it sliding in the first place?

• Most students intuit this. Today we'll explain it.
5–15 min

• Static friction: resists motion BEFORE the object moves. Adjusts itself up to a maximum value.

• Kinetic friction: friction once the object is moving. Usually less than maximum static.

• Both depend on: (1) the normal force pressing surfaces together, and (2) the surface pair (coefficient of friction μ).

• Formulas: f_static ≤ μ_s · N. f_kinetic = μ_k · N. Typically μ_s > μ_k.
15–35 min

• Investigation 1.5 (Friction-on-Cart). Dynamics cart on horizontal track, pulled by force sensor through string. Surface materials can be varied (wax paper, regular paper, sandpaper).

• Pull cart at constant velocity — sensor reading IS the friction force. Vary mass, vary surface, vary speed.

• Calculate μ_k = f / N for each surface. Compare across surfaces. Confirm that μ depends on surface pair, not on speed (within our range).
35–48 min

• Whole-class discussion: friction is what stops everyday objects. Without friction, a hockey puck would slide forever.

• In space, there's no friction. The asteroid doesn't naturally slow down. This is why a small force applied for a long time CAN deflect it — the force adds up without any 'natural' drag.

• Foreshadow Unit 4: this is why the gravity tractor strategy works in principle. You can't 'wear down' the asteroid through friction; you can only push it.
48–50 min • Exit ticket: "Why does space being frictionless make asteroid deflection both easier and harder?"

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Lab data: are coefficients of friction reasonable? (μ_k ≈ 0.1-0.5 for most surfaces.)

• Exit ticket: students should articulate that no friction means small forces accumulate over time (easier deflection), but also that no friction means the asteroid doesn't naturally slow (harder to absorb).

• Vocabulary check: 'static' vs. 'kinetic' friction — used correctly?

MATERIALS & READING

• Vernier force sensors and dynamics carts

• Track and various surface materials (wax paper, regular paper, sandpaper)

• Hanging masses for varying normal force

• Lab handout with data table

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Read Hewitt sections on friction (varies by edition; usually Chapter 5 or 6).

TEACHER NOTES

Friction is a real, present force in everyday life but ABSENT in space. The contrast is the day's pedagogical point. The asteroid analogy: students who internalize 'space has no friction' will understand why even small spacecraft thrust accumulates over months to produce meaningful Δv. This is the basis for gravity tractor and ion engine deflection strategies (Unit 4). Common error: students think friction is always 'bad' (it opposes motion). Push back: friction is what lets you WALK. Without friction, your feet would just slide. Friction enables AND prevents motion, depending on context.

title: Day 18 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 18 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Equilibrium: When Forces Balance

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can identify situations where ΣF = 0, and I can use this condition to find unknown forces.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 17. We've covered net force and friction. Today: special case of zero net force.

How today connects: An object at rest, or moving at constant velocity, has ΣF = 0. Many engineering problems require this condition.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap: F_net = ma. What if a = 0? Then F_net = 0. This is EQUILIBRIUM.

• Equilibrium = forces balance. Object is either at rest OR moving at constant velocity. (Both have a = 0!)
5–20 min

• Practice scenarios with equilibrium:

• - A book at rest on a desk. ΣF_vertical = 0 → Normal = Gravity. (Mass = 1 kg, F_g = 9.8 N, so N = 9.8 N.)

• - A box being pushed at constant velocity on a rough floor. ΣF_horizontal = 0 → applied force = friction. If applied = 20 N, then friction = 20 N (regardless of mass).

• Connect: if a = 0, all forces balance. This works in any number of dimensions.
20–43 min

• Tougher problems: object hanging from two ropes at angles. Or a sign hanging from a wall.

• Students decompose tensions into components, write equilibrium equations (sum_x = 0 and sum_y = 0), solve for unknowns.

• These problems use Day 6 vector skills + Day 18 equilibrium concept.
43–50 min

• Foreshadow Unit 2: in deep space, 2026-XJ is NOT in equilibrium. Solar gravity is constantly accelerating it (slightly). But over short time scales we treat its motion as approximately equilibrium because the acceleration is so small.

• Exit ticket: A 5 kg lamp hangs from a single rope. What's the tension in the rope?

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Equilibrium problems: are students writing ΣF_x = 0 and ΣF_y = 0 explicitly?

• Multi-rope problems: are students decomposing forces correctly?

• Exit ticket: students should answer T = 49 N (m·g for m = 5 kg).

MATERIALS & READING

• Equilibrium Worksheet (6-8 problems of varying difficulty)

• Calculators

• Whiteboards

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Re-read Hewitt sections on equilibrium and Newton's First Law.

TEACHER NOTES

Equilibrium problems are the foundation of statics. They also reinforce vector decomposition skills. The multi-rope problems are HARD — be ready to scaffold. For CPA students, use the heaviest scaffolding possible: provide the diagram, walk through one example carefully, then let them attempt similar problems with guidance. The asteroid is intentionally NOT in equilibrium (solar gravity is always pulling it). But it's CLOSE to equilibrium on short timescales — which is why we treated its velocity as constant in Day 5. This is the bridge to Unit 2.

title: Day 19 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 19 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Workshop and Revision Day

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can identify my own weak areas from Unit 1 work so far and address them before the transfer task.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 18. Today is a stocktaking day — what have we learned so far?

How today connects: Before the transfer task (Days 21-22), each student needs to know where they are. Today they self-assess and target their weak spots.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–10 min

• Stocktaking: review the unit's learning targets. Students rate themselves on each one (0-4 scale, matching the rubric).

• Distribute Unit 1 Learning Target Self-Assessment sheet (lists all Knowledge / Reasoning / Skill targets from the Unit Targets document).

• Students mark each target: not yet (0), still learning (1-2), got it (3-4).
10–35 min

• Differentiated workshop. Based on self-assessments, students choose ONE OR TWO targets to work on:

• - Re-do problems from earlier in the unit (worksheets available)

• - Re-attempt a confusing investigation (if appropriate)

• - Watch a recorded mini-lecture (if available — e.g., Khan Academy on F = ma, vectors, FBDs)

• - Get coaching from teacher (sign up for 5-minute slots)

• - Work with peers who are strong in their weak area

• Goal: each student addresses at least one specific weak spot.
35–48 min

• Brief reconvene: students share ONE thing they figured out today that they hadn't gotten before.

• Whole-class encouragement: this is the moment to fix the weak spots, BEFORE the transfer task.
48–50 min • Exit ticket: "What target do you still feel weakest on? What's your plan to fix it before the transfer task?"

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Self-assessment sheets: do students identify their actual weak spots, or are they overconfident? Compare to your read of their work.

• Workshop choice: are students choosing meaningful work, or socializing? Walk the room.

• Exit ticket: do students have a concrete plan, or are they vague? Vague plans need 1:1 follow-up.

MATERIALS & READING

• Unit 1 Learning Target Self-Assessment sheet (all unit targets)

• Resource folder: worksheets, mini-lecture videos, worked examples

• Sign-up sheet for 5-minute teacher coaching slots (cap at 6 students)

• Vector Recipe Card (extras available)

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Re-read whichever Hewitt chapter your weak area comes from. Practice 3 problems from that chapter.

TEACHER NOTES

RESIST THE URGE TO TEACH TODAY. Today is for students to do their own gap-closing. The teacher's role is COACH, not lecturer. Strategies: (1) For students who are over-confident — gentle probing. "Show me how you'd solve a vector decomposition problem." (2) For students who are under-confident — celebrate their progress. They're often farther along than they think. (3) For students who are genuinely behind — schedule a longer 1:1 conversation about what's missing. This day matters because students who go into the transfer task with unaddressed weak spots will struggle. The day after tomorrow is the transfer task — this is the LAST chance to fix things.

title: Day 20 · Phase 2 — Building the tools
bodyHtml:
DAY 20 · Phase 2 — Building the tools

Synthesis: 2026-XJ Through the Full Physics Lens

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can apply all of Unit 1's tools (vectors, F = ma, FBDs, friction, equilibrium) to the asteroid scenario in an integrated way.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 19, plus the prep work students did yesterday. Tomorrow they receive new disclosure (end-of-Unit-1 data drop) and start the transfer task.

How today connects: Today is the FINAL synthesis day before the transfer task. Students see the full unit applied to the asteroid in one coherent picture.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Today: we use EVERYTHING we've learned to make sense of 2026-XJ.

• Recap: position, displacement, velocity, acceleration, force, vectors, Newton's laws, FBDs, friction, equilibrium.
5–25 min

• Whole-class case study. Walk through the asteroid scenario applying every Unit 1 tool:

• - DRAW the FBD for 2026-XJ in deep space. Forces: solar gravity (dominant), Earth gravity (weaker), other planetary gravity (negligible). No friction.

• - The NET force on the asteroid is roughly solar gravity, pointing toward the Sun.

• - By F = ma, the asteroid accelerates toward the Sun. But that doesn't stop it — it just changes its trajectory.

• - Velocity vector: 8500 m/s at 15° toward Earth. Decompose into components.

• - Predict position in 6 months using constant-velocity approximation (Unit 1 toolkit).

• - Acknowledge the uncertainty in this prediction.

• Have students participate at each step — what's the FBD? What's the net force? What's the acceleration?
25–45 min

• Group problem: students in groups of 3-4 work through a related but slightly different asteroid scenario. (Use modified numbers from the case study.)

• Each group produces: an FBD, a net force calculation, a velocity decomposition, and a position prediction.

• Teacher circulates, supports.
45–50 min

• Closing: tomorrow you receive the official Unit 1 transfer task. Same kind of work — but you'll do it independently and submit it.

• Exit ticket: "What's the one tool from this unit you most need to have ready for the transfer task?"

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Whole-class case study: which steps did students lead well? Which needed teacher support? Note for transfer-task day.

• Group work: did groups produce complete, correct work?

• Exit ticket: students identify their final concern, which becomes their final pre-transfer-task target.

MATERIALS & READING

• Asteroid Synthesis Case Study handout (the worked example)

• Group Problem handout (modified scenario for group work)

• Whiteboards

• Calculators

• Exit ticket slips

Hewitt reading: Light reading tonight — review notes from the unit. Sleep well; tomorrow is the transfer task.

TEACHER NOTES

TODAY IS THE FINAL DRESS REHEARSAL. Students see the whole unit integrated in one example. The whole-class case study is the most important pedagogical move — students should feel that they CAN do this when it comes time to do it themselves tomorrow. Watch for groups that finish quickly — those are the ready ones. Watch for groups that struggle — they need final 1:1 coaching before tomorrow. The exit ticket flags students who are still uncertain; you can reach out to them tonight or first thing tomorrow.

title: Day 21 · Phase 3 — Transfer task
bodyHtml:
DAY 21 · Phase 3 — Transfer task

Unit 1 Transfer Task Day 1: Receive, Plan, Begin

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can read the transfer task carefully, identify what's being asked across the four rubric dimensions, plan my approach, and start the analysis.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Today the end-of-Unit-1 disclosure arrives: refined trajectory data from NASA. The asteroid will make a CLOSE PASS before its final approach. This is also the transfer task scenario.

How today connects: The transfer task IS the asteroid work. Students receive trajectory data and predict where 2026-XJ will be in six months, with uncertainty bounds — exactly what NASA analysts are doing right now (in 2026).

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Brief norms reset: independent work today, but you may ask CLARIFYING questions about the task. You may NOT ask physics-content questions.

• Vector Recipe Card (Day 6) is allowed. Calculator is allowed. No Hewitt.
5–15 min

• Hand out the transfer task: a 4-part task (vectors, position prediction, uncertainty analysis, public-facing paragraph) on the asteroid scenario.

• Students read SILENTLY and INDIVIDUALLY for 10 minutes. No talking yet.

• Write what you NOTICE about the task and what you WONDER (echoing Day 1's protocol — full circle from the start of the year).
15–25 min

• Re-read the task. For each rubric dimension, identify what THIS task is asking you to demonstrate:

• - SCIENCE: what content do I need to use correctly? (Vectors, kinematics, uncertainty)

• - REASONING: what does the task require me to JUSTIFY? (Why this approach? What's the meaning of uncertainty?)

• - COMMUNICATION: what does a clear answer look like? (Show work, label units, organize the response, write a paragraph anyone could read.)

• - TRANSFER-TO-ASTEROID: how do I honestly connect this to the year's question? (The paragraph for the public is where this shows.)
25–45 min

• Build a plan and BEGIN. Sketch a plan: what order will you tackle the parts? Where will you spend most of your time? Where do you predict you'll struggle?

• Then BEGIN. Most students will get through Part 1 (vector decomposition) and start Part 2 (position calculation) today.

• Teacher: walk the room. NO content help. Yes to clarifying questions about what the task is asking.
45–50 min

• Self-check: where am I now? What dimensions have I touched today?

• Bring everything tomorrow. Work stays in the classroom (preserves the integrity of the assessment as transfer).

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Reading and planning quality: are students taking the planning step seriously, or rushing to start?

• Dimension identification: do students correctly identify what each dimension asks of them?

• Pacing: who's behind? Note for tomorrow's morning check-in.

MATERIALS & READING

• Unit 1 Transfer Task (the 4-part document from the Transfer Tasks document)

• 4-Dimensional Rubric (visible at every desk)

• Vector Recipe Card (Day 6)

• Graph paper

• Calculators

• Self-Assessment template (for the planning step)

Hewitt reading: No reading. Sleep well.

TEACHER NOTES

TODAY IS DAY 1 OF THE TRANSFER TASK. The format is identical to every transfer task across the year, so students will recognize this rhythm by Unit 4. Keep work in the room — students should not take it home (prevents outside help, preserves the integrity of the assessment as transfer). The planning step is the most important pedagogical move of Day 1. Without it, students grind through the task without ever stopping to consider what they're being asked. With it, the task becomes manageable. The Day 1 notice/wonder protocol is a nice callback to the unit's beginning.

title: Day 22 · Phase 3 — Transfer task
bodyHtml:
DAY 22 · Phase 3 — Transfer task

Unit 1 Transfer Task Day 2: Complete, Defend, Submit

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGET

I can complete the transfer task, defend my reasoning to a peer using the rubric, revise based on their feedback, and submit work I can be honest about.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What students know about 2026-XJ as today opens: Same as Day 21. The asteroid scenario continues; the close-pass data is the transfer-task scenario.

How today connects: Today the year's first complete piece of asteroid analysis is submitted. The work will inform Unit 2 (gravity & orbits) and ultimately the year-end synthesis.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Retrieve work-in-progress from yesterday.

• Check your Day-21 plan. Adjust if your priorities have shifted.

• Quick triage: what's the one thing you most need to finish in the first 25 minutes?
5–25 min

• Independent work. Most students should complete Part 3 (uncertainty analysis) and start Part 4 (the public-facing paragraph).

• Teacher walks for clarifying questions only. No content help.
25–40 min

• PEER DEFENSE (the load-bearing assessment move).

• Pair up (random or pre-assigned).

• Person A explains their entire transfer task to Person B in 5 minutes. Person B holds the rubric and listens.

• Person B then asks 2-3 questions, scoring A on each dimension as they hear the answers. Person B writes one specific note: "Your Transfer-to-Asteroid rating from me would be X because..."

• Switch roles. 5 minutes B explains, then 2-3 min A questions.
40–48 min

• Final revision based on peer feedback. Maximum: ~5 minutes — this is polish, not rebuild.

• Submit. Final reflection on packet: how did you feel? What's the one dimension you most wish you'd worked on more?

• Final 4-dimension self-rating: where do YOU think you scored across Science / Reasoning / Communication / Transfer-to-Asteroid?
48–50 min

• Submit transfer task + 4-dimension self-rating + brief reflection.

• Tomorrow: Unit 2 (Why doesn't it just orbit us forever?) begins. New unit, new puzzle piece, same asteroid.

FORMATIVE CHECKS (how teacher knows what students are getting)

• Peer defense quality: are students using the rubric language? Or just being polite?

• Self-rating accuracy: students who rate themselves accurately are showing meta-cognitive growth — track this across the year.

• Submission completeness: any blanks? Any clearly rushed sections? Note for grading conversations.

MATERIALS & READING

• Same as Day 21

• Final submission folders (or however your gradebook intake works)

• Self-Reflection form (final 4-dim self-rating, one-line reflection)

Hewitt reading: Done with Unit 1. Read Hewitt's introduction to gravitation (Chapter 9) tonight — Unit 2 begins tomorrow.

TEACHER NOTES

SCORING THE TASK: use the four-dimension rubric from the Transfer Tasks document. Score each dimension 0-4 INDEPENDENTLY. Do NOT average. Report the profile (e.g., 'Science 3 / Reasoning 3 / Transfer-to-Asteroid 2 / Communication 4'). Convert to letter grade using the lowest-dimension rule from the YAG: this student gets a B (the 2 in Transfer-to-Asteroid reveals a real growth area). Comment on the report card should name the specific dimension that's the growth edge. Students may revise within 2 weeks (one-level move on any dimension; must demonstrate the change with new evidence).
